title: Preparing Cloth for Marbling
subtitle: Mordanting natural fibres for transfer from the size surface
excerpt: Cloth can be marbled using the same carrageenan size and acrylic colours as paper, but the mordanting step requires different alum concentration and application method. Natural fibres accept the treatment; synthetic fibres do not. The resulting marbled cloth has uses in bookbinding, framing, and scrapbooking.
sourceNotes: Halfer, Josef. Progress of the Marbling Art. English translation. Buffalo, NY: O.H. Neumann, 1893/1894. Section on marbling on fabric.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of treating cloth with an alum solution to make it receptive to marbling paint. Cloth requires a higher alum concentration than paper because the fibre structure is more resistant to mordant penetration.
The prepared alum solution used to pre-treat cloth or paper before marbling. Concentration is adjusted to the material: higher for cloth, lower for paper.

Plant-based (cotton, linen, silk) or animal-based (wool, silk) textile fibres that accept alum mordanting and bond with acrylic marbling paint. Synthetic fibres such as polyester do not mordant and will not hold the pattern.

attrs.heading: Marbling chemistry safety
attrs.body: Alum is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying the mordant and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is slippery; wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

content.text: Natural fibres only

Marbling paint transfers to cloth through the same mordant mechanism as paper: the alum pre-treatment creates a surface charge that bonds the acrylic binder in the paint to the substrate. This mechanism depends on the hydroxyl groups present in cellulose and protein fibres.
A
natural fibre

cloth, cotton, linen, silk, or a cotton-linen blend, will accept the mordant and hold the paint after washing. Polyester, nylon, and most blends containing more than 20% synthetic content will not mordant properly. The paint may transfer initially but will wash off or peel after the cloth is rinsed.

Check the label before purchasing cloth for marbling. For bookbinding, a tightly woven cotton cambric or a cotton-linen book cloth (without the paper backing found on commercial bookbinding cloth) is ideal. For decorative projects, a lightweight cotton muslin gives a soft, slightly translucent marbled result.
Alum concentration for cloth
The
alum sizing
for cloth requires a higher alum concentration than for paper. For paper, 25 to 30 g per litre is standard. For
cloth mordanting
, increase this to 40 to 50 g per litre. The cloth fibre structure absorbs more alum than a paper surface does, and a lower concentration will produce inconsistent paint adhesion.
Dissolve the alum in warm water and allow to cool fully before application. Hot alum solution can damage delicate fabrics and may interfere with the mordanting process.
Application method

Cloth requires more thorough wetting than paper. Lay the cloth flat on a clean plastic sheet. Apply the alum solution with a sponge, working it into the cloth with a circular pressing motion rather than a brushing motion. Brushing displaces the alum toward the surface; pressing forces it through the weave.
Turn the cloth and apply alum to the reverse side. The cloth should be thoroughly damp, not soaked to the point of dripping. Wring out any excess.

Allow to dry completely. Cloth can be pin-dried flat or hung; it should not be crumpled or bunched while damp or the alum will distribute unevenly. Once dry, the treated cloth is stiff to the touch: this is normal and is caused by the alum crystals in the weave. The stiffness relaxes after marbling and rinsing.
Preventing the cloth from sinking

Cloth sinks more readily than paper when placed on the size surface. The weight of the material and the capillary action of the weave both pull it down into the size. Several precautions reduce this risk.

First, the cloth must be fully dry before marbling. Damp cloth absorbs size immediately and sinks. Second, the carrageenan size should be slightly thicker than for paper marbling: 1.5 to 2 g per litre rather than 1 g per litre. The denser size provides more surface support.

Third, lay the cloth onto the size quickly and with minimal hesitation. Hesitating while partially in contact allows the leading edge to sink before the whole cloth is placed. The method is similar to paper: hold the cloth at two opposite corners, allow it to bow slightly in the middle, and touch the centre to the size first, then release the corners so the cloth lies flat. This centre-first approach traps no air and minimises the time any edge spends sinking.

Leave on the size for 30 to 45 seconds, the same as paper. If the cloth is floating correctly it will lift cleanly with the pattern attached. If it has sunk, it will come up with size on the upper surface as well as the underside; this excess size must be rinsed off before it dries, as dried carrageenan is difficult to remove from cloth.
Lifting and rinsing
Lift the cloth from one corner in a steady, even pull. Do not drag it along the size surface; lift vertically. The pattern should transfer cleanly.

Rinse the marbled side under cold, slow-running water. Do not agitate or scrub. The rinse removes the carrageenan size and loosely adhering paint. The fixed paint will remain. Continue rinsing until the water running off is clear.

Pin the cloth flat to dry. Do not use a tumble dryer or press with a hot iron immediately after rinsing; the paint needs to cure at room temperature for at least 24 hours before heat is applied. After curing, many acrylic marbling paints can be heat-set with an iron on a medium setting (check the specific paint's instructions); heat-setting significantly improves wash fastness.
Uses for marbled cloth

Marbled cloth has been used in bookbinding for centuries, most commonly as covering material for the spine area or as a decorative element on the boards. A lightweight marbled cotton makes an excellent full-cloth case for a small journal or notebook. The cloth is cut and adhered with PVA, and the marbled surface prints cleanly without the ink lifting during adhesion.

In scrapbooking, marbled fabric strips and patches provide a distinct alternative to marbled paper and add tactile interest to pages. The soft edges of fabric patches, left cut rather than torn, suit layouts where a precise edge is not required.

Frame marbled cloth the same way you would frame any textile: UV-protective glass, acid-free mounting board, and no adhesive on the cloth itself. A marbled silk makes a strong framed piece. The sheen of the silk adds a glow to the pattern that cotton cannot match.
